perturbation.original_text: (c) Payment of License Fees shall be made in Canadian funds in arrears within thirty (30) days following each calendar month during the Term in which Licensed Programs were exhibited hereunder and shall be accompanied by a statement of account showing the calculation of the License Fees.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Payment of License Fees shall be made in Canadian funds in arrears within ninety (90) days following each calendar quarter during the Term in which Licensed Programs were exhibited hereunder and shall be accompanied by a statement of account showing the calculation of the License Fees.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment frequency to quarterly and extending the payment deadline to 90 days may violate prompt payment laws depending on the jurisdiction and the nature of the agreement. In many jurisdictions, payment for services rendered must be made more promptly, especially if the agreement is interpreted as an employment or contractor agreement. The longer delay could trigger penalties or interest under applicable provincial laws in Canada.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Construction Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.30, s. 6
perturbation.law_citation: Ontario Construction Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.30, s. 6
perturbation.law_explanation: The Ontario Construction Act mandates prompt payment terms for construction projects. While this agreement isn't explicitly about construction, delayed payment terms could be challenged if the services provided are related to construction or improvement of property. The modified 90-day quarterly payment schedule could be deemed unreasonable and violate the spirit of prompt payment legislation, even if it doesn't directly fall under the Construction Act's scope. Section 6 of the Act provides the details for proper invoices and timelines for payment.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment terms from 'within thirty (30) days following each calendar month' to 'within ninety (90) days following each calendar quarter.' This change contradicts the prompt payment principles outlined in the Ontario Construction Act, as the Perturbation Explanation suggests potential violations of prompt payment laws. Although the Law Explanation acknowledges the agreement might not explicitly fall under the Construction Act, it argues the delayed payment schedule could be deemed unreasonable and violate the spirit of prompt payment legislation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the Ontario Construction Act, includes Part I.1 regarding 'PROMPT PAYMENT,' specifically sections 6.4, 6.5, and 6.6, which outline payment deadlines from owner to contractor and from contractor to subcontractor. While the snippets do not specify the exact time frame considered 'prompt,' the change to quarterly payments with a 90-day delay significantly extends the payment period, creating an inconsistency with the intent of prompt payment legislation. The act provides the framework for challenging payment delays, and the changed text introduces payment terms that deviate substantially from those expected in construction-related agreements, increasing risk of non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 18. ROGERS' TERMINATION RIGHTS. Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, Rogers may terminate this Agreement, at any time, upon sixty (60) days' prior written notice to Licensor.
perturbation.changed_text: 18. ROGERS' TERMINATION RIGHTS. Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, Rogers may terminate this Agreement, at any time, upon ten (10) days' prior written notice to Licensor.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period from 60 days to 10 days could be deemed unreasonable and potentially violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, especially if the Licensor is significantly reliant on the agreement for its revenue. This is also more likely if the contract is ongoing, rather than for a fixed term.
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the termination notice to 10 days could violate the implied duty of good faith under UCC 2-309(3), which requires reasonable notification before termination to allow the other party sufficient time to seek a substitute arrangement. Ten days might not be considered a reasonable amount of time, thereby potentially leading to legal challenges, especially if the licensor faces substantial losses due to the abrupt termination.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 60 to 10 days, which could violate the implied duty of good faith, as stated in the Law Explanation. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations do not align with legal requirements, potentially making the contractual terms unenforceable. Although no snippet directly mentions UCC 2-309(3), the Law Explanation indicates that 10 days may be deemed unreasonable under this provision, particularly if the Licensor faces substantial losses due to abrupt termination. The general discussion of the UCC in Scraped Law Snippet 2, although not directly on point, generally supports that contracts must remain in accordance with the UCC provisions.
